section.title: The Ordination of Aaron
section.sectionRationale: The book turns from law to narrative here, the ordination account. Moses carries out, verse by verse, exactly what the LORD commanded in Exodus 29, washing, clothing, anointing, and offering sacrifices to consecrate Aaron and his sons as Israel's first priests.
section.thesis: Moses ordains Aaron and his sons exactly as the LORD commanded, through washing, clothing, anointing, and a sequence of sacrifices whose blood consecrates the altar, the priests, and their garments, teaching that access to serve a holy God is granted only through God's own prescribed way, followed in every detail.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the assembly gathered and Aaron washed and clothed; the bull and two rams offered, blood applied to ear, thumb, and toe; the ordination meal eaten at the tent entrance; and the seven days of remaining there in obedience.

units.label: Washed and clothed before the assembly

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Take Aaron and his sons, their garments, the anointing oil, the bull of the sin offering, the two rams, and the basket of unleavened bread,
and assemble the whole congregation at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.”
So Moses did as the LORD had commanded him, and the assembly gathered at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
And Moses said to them, “This is what the LORD has commanded to be done.”
Then Moses presented Aaron and his sons and washed them with water.
He put the tunic on Aaron, tied the sash around him, clothed him with the robe, and put the ephod on him. He tied the woven band of the ephod around him and fastened it to him.
Then he put the breastpiece on him and placed the Urim and Thummim in the breastpiece.
Moses also put the turban on Aaron's head and set the gold plate, the holy diadem, on the front of the turban, as the LORD had commanded him.
Next, Moses took the anointing oil and anointed the tabernacle and everything in it; and so he {{qadash|consecrated}} them.
He sprinkled some of the oil on the altar seven times, anointing the altar and all its utensils, and the basin with its stand, to consecrate them.
He also poured some of the anointing oil on Aaron's head and anointed him to consecrate him.
Then Moses presented Aaron's sons, put tunics on them, wrapped sashes around them, and tied headbands on them, just as the LORD had commanded him.

units.label: The bull and the two rams
units.note: blood on ear, thumb, and toe

verses.text: Moses then brought the bull near for the sin offering, and Aaron and his sons laid their hands on its head.

Moses slaughtered the bull, took some of the blood, and applied it with his finger to all four horns of the altar, purifying the altar. He poured out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar and consecrated it so that {{kipper|atonement}} could be made on it.
Moses also took all the fat that was on the entrails, the lobe of the liver, and both kidneys and their fat, and burned it all on the altar.
But the bull with its hide, flesh, and dung he burned outside the camp, as the LORD had commanded him.
Then Moses presented the ram for the burnt offering, and Aaron and his sons laid their hands on its head.
Moses slaughtered the ram and sprinkled the blood on all sides of the altar.
He cut the ram into pieces and burned the head, the pieces, and the fat.
He washed the entrails and legs with water and burned the entire ram on the altar as a burnt offering, a pleasing aroma, an offering made by fire to the LORD, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
After that, Moses presented the other ram, the ram of {{milluim|ordination}}, and Aaron and his sons laid their hands on its head.
Moses slaughtered the ram and took some of its blood and put it on Aaron's right earlobe, on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.

Moses also presented Aaron's sons and put some of the blood on their right earlobes, on the thumbs of their right hands, and on the big toes of their right feet. Then he sprinkled the blood on all sides of the altar.
And Moses took the fat, the fat tail, all the fat that was on the entrails, the lobe of the liver, and both kidneys with their fat, as well as the right thigh.

And from the basket of unleavened bread that was before the LORD, he took one cake of unleavened bread, one cake of bread made with oil, and one wafer, and he placed them on the fat portions and on the right thigh.
He put all these in the hands of Aaron and his sons and waved them before the LORD as a wave offering.
Then Moses took these from their hands and burned them on the altar with the burnt offering. This was an ordination offering, a pleasing aroma, an offering made by fire to the LORD.
He also took the breast, Moses' portion of the ram of ordination, and waved it before the LORD as a wave offering, as the LORD had commanded him.

Next, Moses took some of the anointing oil and some of the blood that was on the altar and sprinkled them on Aaron and his garments, and on his sons and their garments. So he consecrated Aaron and his garments, as well as Aaron's sons and their garments.

And Moses said to Aaron and his sons, “Boil the meat at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and eat it there with the bread that is in the basket of ordination offerings, as I commanded, saying, ‘Aaron and his sons are to eat it.’
Then you must burn up the remainder of the meat and bread.

units.label: Seven days at the entrance
units.note: remain, and keep the LORD's charge

verses.text: You must not go outside the entrance to the Tent of Meeting for seven days, until the days of your ordination are complete; for it will take seven days to ordain you.
What has been done today has been commanded by the LORD in order to make atonement on your behalf.
You must remain at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting day and night for seven days and keep the LORD's charge so that you will not die, for this is what I have been commanded.”
So Aaron and his sons did everything the LORD had commanded through Moses.

qadash.gloss: To consecrate, set apart as holy. Moses uses this verb repeatedly, of the tabernacle, the altar, Aaron, and his garments, showing that ordination is fundamentally an act of God setting people and objects apart exclusively for His service.

milluim.gloss: Ordination, literally a filling, related to the idiom to fill the hand, meaning to install someone in office. The ram of ordination and the offering placed in Aaron's hands enact this idiom literally, filling his hands as he is installed as priest.

kipper.gloss: To atone, to cover. Verse 34 states plainly that this entire elaborate ritual was commanded in order to make atonement, showing that even the priest himself, before he can mediate atonement for others, must first be atoned for and consecrated.

basic.context: This chapter narrates Moses actually carrying out the ordination ritual God had prescribed back in Exodus 29, washing Aaron and his sons, clothing them in priestly garments, anointing the tabernacle and the altar, and offering a bull and two rams whose blood consecrates the altar and is applied to the priests' ear, thumb, and toe. The chapter repeatedly notes that Moses did exactly as the LORD had commanded, closing with seven days that Aaron and his sons must remain at the tent entrance.
basic.hermeneutics: This narrates a specific, historical, one time old covenant ordination; it is not a pattern for ordaining Christian ministers today. Its abiding truth is that access to serve a holy God is never self appointed; it comes only through God's own prescribed way, followed exactly, and even the mediator himself needs atonement before he can mediate for others.
basic.originalLanguages: The recurring verb qadash, to consecrate, marks the tabernacle, the altar, Aaron, and his garments as all set apart exclusively for God's service. The ram of milluim, ordination, literally a filling, enacts the idiom to fill the hand, installing Aaron in office, and verse 34 states plainly that the whole ritual existed so that kaphar, atonement, could be made, even for the priest himself.
basic.typology: Blood applied to Aaron's ear, thumb, and toe consecrates his whole capacity to hear, to act, and to walk in God's service, foreshadowing the whole life consecration of believers under Christ. That Aaron himself required atonement before serving points forward to Christ, the one priest who needed no atonement for Himself, since He was without sin.
basic.christ: Hebrews contrasts Aaron, who needed washing, clothing, and blood applied before he could serve, and who had to offer first for his own sins, with Jesus, our great High Priest, holy, innocent, undefiled, who had no need to offer for Himself, Hebrews 7:26-27. Where Aaron's ordination required seven days of confinement and repeated atonement, Christ's priesthood is permanent and unrepeatable, secured by an oath, Hebrews 7:20-21.
basic.god: God requires an elaborate, costly process before even His chosen priest can serve Him, showing how seriously He regards His own holiness; access is never casual, not even for Aaron. Yet the whole process exists, as verse 34 states, in order to make atonement, revealing a God who provides, at cost, the very way for guilty people to serve and approach Him.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 7:26-27 contrasts Christ's sinless priesthood with Aaron's need to offer first for his own sins. Hebrews 7:20-21 notes that Christ became priest with an oath, unlike the Levitical priests, marking His priesthood as permanent. Romans 12:1 calls believers to offer their whole selves, echoing the whole person consecration pictured in the blood on ear, thumb, and toe.
basic.oneStory: Aaron's ordination shows that no one, not even Israel's first high priest, could serve a holy God without first being washed, clothed, and atoned for through blood God Himself prescribed. This points forward to Christ, the sinless High Priest who needed no atonement for Himself and who, by His own blood, permanently consecrates all who come to God through Him.

context.literary: This chapter is the narrative fulfillment of Exodus 29's instructions, told almost verse for verse in the same order, with the refrain just as the LORD had commanded him repeated throughout to underline Moses' exact obedience.
context.historical: This ordination inaugurated Israel's entire priesthood; every subsequent high priest in Israel's history traced his consecration back to this pattern, first enacted here by Moses on Aaron and his sons.
context.canonical: The specific details of this ordination, blood on ear, thumb, and toe, the seven days of confinement, and the priest's own need for atonement, become vocabulary the book of Hebrews draws on extensively to show both the old priesthood's limits and Christ's surpassing priesthood.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the actual, historical fulfillment of God's ordination instructions for Aaron and his sons, emphasizing throughout that Moses did exactly as the LORD had commanded, establishing the pattern for Israel's priesthood.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This narrates a historical, one time ordination event for Israel's old covenant priesthood; it does not prescribe a ritual pattern for the church to reenact. Its abiding principle is that service to God is granted only on His terms, and that even mediators of atonement themselves need atonement.

debates.question: Why must Aaron and his sons remain at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting for seven full days, verses 33 to 35?

views.label: A period of completed consecration
views.case: Seven, the number of completeness in the Torah's symbolism, matches the seven days needed to fully consecrate priest and altar together, a process that could not be rushed or shortened.

views.label: A test of obedience before full service began
views.case: Others note the stated warning, so that you will not die, verse 35, suggesting the seven days also functioned as a period proving the priests' willingness to obey exactly, before they were entrusted with the weightier task of mediating for the whole nation.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that this waiting period reinforced that priestly service could not be assumed casually or quickly, but required sustained, careful obedience to God's exact instruction.

study.originalLanguages: The verb qadash, to consecrate, marks everyone and everything touched by this ritual, the tabernacle, the altar, Aaron, and his sons, as exclusively set apart for God. The ram of milluim, ordination, enacts the ancient idiom to fill the hand, literally installing Aaron in office, and verse 34 states directly that this whole elaborate process existed so that kaphar, atonement, might be made even for the priest himself before he served others.

typology.type: Blood applied to ear, thumb, and toe
typology.fulfillment: The whole person consecration of believers, hearing, doing, and walking, offered to God through Christ, Romans 12:1.

typology.type: Aaron's own need for atonement before serving others
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the sinless High Priest who required no atonement for Himself, Hebrews 7:26-27.

typology.type: The seven days of ordination before full priestly service began
typology.fulfillment: The completeness and permanence of Christ's priesthood, secured once for all by God's own oath, Hebrews 7:20-21.

study.christ: Hebrews 7:26-27 draws a direct contrast between Aaron, who had to be washed and offer first for his own sins before serving others, and Jesus, holy, innocent, undefiled, who needed no such preparation, since He offered Himself once for all. Where Aaron's ordination required repeated blood and seven days of confinement, Christ's priesthood, secured by God's own oath, Hebrews 7:20-21, never needs to be repeated or renewed.
study.god: God's holiness demands an elaborate, exacting process before even His appointed priest can serve, showing that access to Him is never casual or self assumed. Yet the entire ritual exists, as the text plainly states, in order to make atonement, revealing a God who Himself provides, at real cost, the way for guilty people to draw near and serve Him.

crossReferences.note: Christ, unlike Aaron, needed no atonement for His own sin before He could offer Himself for others.
Christ became priest by God's own oath, marking His priesthood as permanent, unlike the repeated Levitical ordinations.
Believers are called to offer their whole selves as a living sacrifice, echoing the whole person consecration pictured in blood applied to ear, thumb, and toe.

study.oneStory: Aaron's ordination shows that even Israel's first high priest could not serve a holy God without first being washed, clothed, and atoned for through blood God Himself prescribed in exact detail. This whole costly process anticipates Christ, the sinless and permanent High Priest, who required no atonement for Himself and whose single sacrifice fully and finally consecrates all who come to God through Him.

quiz.q: What phrase recurs throughout this chapter, emphasizing Moses' obedience?

options.text: Just as the LORD had commanded him.
options.note: This phrase, or a close variant, appears repeatedly, verses 4, 9, 13, 17, 21, 29, and 36.

options.text: According to the wisdom of Moses.
options.note: The text consistently credits obedience to the LORD's command, not Moses' own wisdom or initiative.

options.text: As the elders of Israel had decided.
options.note: The chapter attributes every action to the LORD's command given through Moses, not the elders' decision.

options.text: No such phrase appears.
options.note: This phrase is one of the chapter's most prominent and repeated features.

quiz.q: Where was the blood of the ordination ram applied to Aaron and his sons?

options.text: Their right earlobe, the thumb of their right hand, and the big toe of their right foot.
options.note: Verses 23 to 24 specify this exact, threefold application of blood.

options.text: Only their foreheads.
options.note: The gold plate, not blood, was placed on the forehead area via the turban, chapter 8:9; the ordination blood went to ear, thumb, and toe.

options.text: Their hands only.
options.note: The blood was applied to ear, thumb, and toe together, not the hands alone.

options.text: No blood was applied to their bodies.
options.note: Verses 23 to 24 explicitly describe this application of blood.

quiz.q: What did Moses do with the anointing oil and blood from the altar in verse 30?

options.text: He sprinkled them on Aaron and his garments, and on his sons and their garments, consecrating them.
options.note: Verse 30 describes exactly this act of consecration.

options.text: He poured them out entirely at the base of the altar.
options.note: That describes an earlier part of the ritual, verse 15; verse 30 describes oil and blood sprinkled on the priests and their garments.

options.text: He kept them in a container for future use.
options.note: The oil and blood in verse 30 were used immediately to consecrate Aaron, his sons, and their garments.

options.text: Nothing further was done with them.
options.note: Verse 30 explicitly describes this further use of oil and blood.

quiz.q: How long were Aaron and his sons required to remain at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting?

options.text: Seven days, day and night, keeping the LORD's charge.
options.note: Verses 33 and 35 both specify this seven day period.

options.text: One day only.
options.note: The text specifies a full seven days, not a single day.

options.text: Forty days.
options.note: Forty days describes other periods in the Torah, such as Moses' time on Sinai, not this ordination period.

options.text: No time period is specified.
options.note: Verses 33 and 35 give an explicit seven day requirement.

quiz.q: What warning is given regarding the seven days at the tent entrance, verse 35?

options.text: That they must remain there so that they will not die.
options.note: Verse 35 explicitly gives this warning tied to obedience during the ordination period.

options.text: That failure to remain would simply delay their ordination by another week.
options.note: The stated consequence in verse 35 is death, a far more severe warning than mere delay.

options.text: No warning of any consequence is given.
options.note: Verse 35 explicitly states a warning.

options.text: That they would lose their priestly garments if they left.
options.note: The stated consequence is death, not the loss of garments.

questions: Everything in this chapter happened exactly as the LORD commanded, with no improvisation from Moses or Aaron. Where in your own life or worship are you tempted to improvise rather than follow God's way exactly?

Blood was applied to Aaron's ear, thumb, and toe, consecrating his whole person, what he heard, did, and where he walked. Which of these three areas of your life most needs to be brought fully under God's ownership?

Even Aaron, the one who would mediate atonement for others, first needed atonement made for himself. Where do you still try to serve or help others spiritually without first receiving what only Christ can give you?

Aaron and his sons had to remain at the tent entrance for seven full days, unable to leave, under threat of death if they disobeyed. What does this teach you about the seriousness with which God regards obedience to His specific instructions?

Christ, unlike Aaron, needed no atonement for Himself before serving as our priest. How does resting in His finished, sinless priesthood change the way you approach God today, compared to trying to make yourself worthy first?

translationNotes.note: The gold plate, the holy diadem inscribed Holy to the LORD, described fully in Exodus 28:36-38, marked Aaron's forehead as consecrated and able to bear the guilt connected with Israel's holy gifts.

The ram of ordination translates the Hebrew milluim, literally a filling, referring to the ancient idiom to fill the hand, meaning to install someone in office, enacted concretely when the offering is placed directly into Aaron's hands, verse 27.

For it will take seven days to ordain you reflects a fixed period of consecration; seven, a number associated with completeness throughout the Torah, marking this as a full and thorough setting apart for priestly service.
